pattern: Counting ways to lay down a fixed number of non-overlapping segments on a line, where segments are even allowed to touch endpoints, unfolds into a simple two-state running count as you sweep the points left to right.
hints: Think of sweeping the points from left to right one at a time, and track two running counts: sets that are already fully closed, and configurations where the most recent segment currently ends exactly at the point you just reached.

At each new point, the 'closed' count simply carries forward from either kind of state at the previous point, while the 'ends here' count can come from stretching a segment that already ended at the previous point, or from starting a brand-new segment out of a state that was previously closed or itself ending at the previous point -- segments are free to share endpoints.

Maintain these two running arrays (indexed by how many segments have been placed so far) as you iterate through all n points, take everything modulo 1e9+7, and read off the fully-closed count for exactly k segments after processing the last point.

edge_inputs.description: statement example, four points and two segments
statement example, three points and one segment
statement example, thirty points and seven segments (exercises the modulo reduction)
minimum n and minimum k -- only one possible segment
maximum n with the maximum possible k (k = n - 1) -- forces a unique back-to-back tiling
k = n - 1 on a smaller n -- still only one way to tile every gap, regardless of scale
many points but only a single segment required -- lots of placement choices, easy to undercount
maximum n with the minimum k -- large point count, few segments, tests scalability of the running counts

stress.description: random n across the full range with a random valid k for it
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = rng.randint(2, 1000)
    k = rng.randint(1, n - 1)
    return [n, k]

stress.description: a second random spread, skewed toward larger n by using size as a lower bound
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    lo = max(2, min(999, size))
    n = rng.randint(lo, 1000)
    k = rng.randint(1, n - 1)
    return [n, k]
